lessons.title: Soil basics: what plants actually need

blocks.markdown:
Healthy soil is **three things at once**: a reservoir of water, a pantry of nutrients, and a structure that lets roots breathe.

Most plant problems trace back to one of these being out of balance. Before you reach for fertiliser, ask which of the three is actually missing.

- **Water** moves through pore spaces between particles
- **Nutrients** cling to organic matter and clay
- **Air** fills the gaps roots need for oxygen
blocks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 120" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="0" y="0" width="200" height="40" fill="#8B5E3C"/><rect x="0" y="40" width="200" height="40" fill="#A9744F"/><rect x="0" y="80" width="200" height="40" fill="#C9A36A"/><text x="100" y="25" font-size="12" fill="#ffffff" text-anchor="middle">Topsoil</text><text x="100" y="65" font-size="12" fill="#ffffff" text-anchor="middle">Subsoil</text><text x="100" y="105" font-size="12" fill="#3A2A1A" text-anchor="middle">Bedrock</text></svg>
blocks.caption: The three soil horizons most houseplants and garden beds draw from.

cards.front: What three roles does soil play at once?
cards.back: A water reservoir, a nutrient pantry, and an airy structure for roots to breathe.

cards.front: Where do nutrients cling in soil?
cards.back: To organic matter and clay particles - which is why compost improves nutrient holding.

cards.front: Why do roots need air gaps?
cards.back: Roots respire - they need oxygen. Waterlogged soil with no air gaps suffocates them.

questions.stem: A plant is wilting even though the soil is constantly wet. What's the most likely problem?
options: Roots are suffocating from lack of air
The soil has too many nutrients
questions.explanation: Constantly wet soil fills the air gaps roots need to breathe. The wilting is from oxygen-starved roots, not thirst - so adding water makes it worse.

questions.stem: Which amendment most improves a soil's ability to hold nutrients?
questions.explanation: Organic matter (compost) gives nutrients sites to cling to. Sand, gravel, and perlite improve drainage but hold few nutrients.

lessons.title: Light: reading the sun in your space

blocks.markdown:
Plants are sold with labels like *full sun* or *partial shade*. Those words map to **hours of direct light per day**:

- **Full sun** = 6+ hours
- **Partial sun/shade** = 3-6 hours
- **Full shade** = under 3 hours

Watch your space across a day before deciding what to plant.

attributes.label: Lifecycle
values: Complete one full life cycle in a single season, then die.
Live three or more years, returning each spring from the same roots.

attributes.label: Effort
values: Replanted each year - more work, but a clean slate every spring.
Plant once and let them settle in; light pruning and division over time.

attributes.label: Bloom
values: Long, continuous bloom across the warm months.
Shorter bloom windows; many perennials flower for just a few weeks.

blocks.title: A gardening year in the north

events.title: Plan and start seeds indoors
events.description: Map the beds, order seeds, start slow growers like peppers and tomatoes 8 to 10 weeks before last frost.

events.title: Water, mulch, harvest
events.description: Deep, infrequent watering beats shallow daily watering. Mulch to keep moisture and suppress weeds.

events.title: Clean up and amend the soil

blocks.markdown: Different plants want different watering. **Read the leaves and the soil**, not the calendar.

attributes.label: Frequency
values: Every 2-3 weeks; let soil dry completely.
Twice weekly; soil should feel just-damp.
Once weekly when established; deep soak.

attributes.label: Volume
values: Small - just enough to wet the root zone.
Moderate - until you see water at the drainage holes.

attributes.label: Warning sign
values: Crispy edges = underwatering; yellow drop = over.
Wilting late afternoon is normal; morning wilt is trouble.

lessons.title: How plants drink, and what can go wrong

blocks.markdown:
Water uptake is the single most common cause of houseplant trouble - either too little, or so much that roots can't breathe.

Understanding the route water takes through a plant makes both directions diagnosable at a glance.

blocks.title: How water travels through a plant
blocks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 140" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="20" y1="100" x2="180" y2="100" stroke="#5C4A2E" stroke-width="2"/><g data-step="1"><path d="M100 100 L80 120 M100 100 L120 120 M100 100 L100 130 M100 100 L70 115 M100 100 L130 115" stroke="#B8895F" stroke-width="2" fill="none"/><circle cx="75" cy="120" r="3" fill="#B8895F"/><circle cx="125" cy="120" r="3" fill="#B8895F"/></g><g data-step="2"><rect x="96" y="45" width="8" height="55" fill="#4A7A2A"/></g><g data-step="3"><ellipse cx="78" cy="50" rx="20" ry="10" fill="#5DA13B"/><ellipse cx="122" cy="50" rx="20" ry="10" fill="#5DA13B"/><ellipse cx="100" cy="32" rx="22" ry="12" fill="#6FB849"/></g></svg>

steps.label: Step 1: roots draw water from the soil via osmosis.
Step 2: capillary action and root pressure push water up the stem.
Step 3: leaves transpire - water exits through stomata, pulling more up the chain.

blocks.caption: Tap Next step to reveal each stage.

blocks.alt: Tomato leaf with brown, concentric-ring spots characteristic of early blight.
blocks.caption: Early blight on a tomato leaf.

blocks.title: Read the symptoms
blocks.context: Your tomato plant's **lower leaves** show brown spots with concentric rings. The upper canopy looks healthy. You've been watering daily, from above, with a watering can.

questions.explanation: Concentric rings on lower leaves are textbook early blight. The upper canopy is unaffected because the fungus spreads from soil splash, climbing upward over time.

questions.stem: Which single change to care would most reduce recurrence?
options: Move the plant into deeper shade
Water from the base instead of overhead
questions.explanation: Overhead watering splashes fungal spores from soil onto the lower leaves. Watering at the base keeps foliage dry and breaks the transmission cycle.
